file_name: PSGT Agreement NDA New Supplier Non Disclosure Template DC.txt

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of the Services for any reas on or at the written request of a Disclosing Party at any time, the Supplier shall destroy (and promptly deliver a certification of such destruction) or promptly deliver to each Disclosing Party, or to the requesting Disclosing Party as the case may be, all tangible embodiments of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in the Supplier’s or its employees’ possession, including but no t limited to copies, notes, packages, pictures, diagrams, computer memory media and all other tangible embodiments of, or materials containing, any portion of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of the Services for any reason or at the written request of a Disclosing Party, the Supplier will have 180 days to destroy (and deliver a certification of such destruction) or deliver to each Disclosing Party, all tangible embodiments of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in the Supplier’s or its employees’ possession.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the destruction timeframe to 180 days could conflict with laws requiring prompt destruction of sensitive data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FTC Safeguards Rule requires financial institutions to develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive information security program. Delaying the destruction of confidential information for 180 days after termination could be seen as failing to protect the security and confidentiality of customer information, thus violating the Safeguards Rule.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly' to '180 days' for destroying confidential information contradicts the FTC Safeguards Rule, which mandates maintaining a comprehensive information security program. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 directs us to 16 CFR Part 314, which covers safeguarding customer information, reinforcing the need for immediate and secure handling of sensitive data. The 180-day delay introduces a significant risk of data breach or misuse, undermining the rule's objective of protecting customer information security.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, consents, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed given and received when sent by first class mail at any general or branch post office enclosed in a registered or certified postpaid envelope, addressed to the Parties at the addresses set fort h at the beginning of this Agreement, with a copy to the attention of the respective Parties as follows: CGSH: Purchasing & Supply Group Supplier: [e.g. General Counsel,], or to such changed address as su ch Parties may have fixed by notice; provided, however, that any notice of change of address shall be effective only up on receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, consents, demands, and other communications provided for by this Agreement must be delivered in person to be considered valid. No other form of communication will be accepted, regardless of circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting acceptable communication to 'in person' only could contradict state laws requiring accommodations for people with disabilities. Registered agent service is usually sufficient for formal legal notices in DC.
perturbation.law_explanation: The ADA requires businesses to provide effective communication to individuals with disabilities. Restricting communication to 'in person' only, without offering alternatives, could discriminate against individuals with hearing, speech, or other communication-related disabilities, and fail to provide auxiliary aids and services to ensure effective communication.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ADA Requirements: Effective Communication | ADA.gov Skip to page navigation Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Table of contents Print this page ADA Requirements: Effective Communication Last updated: February 28, 2020 The Department of Justice published revised final regulations implementing the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) for title II (State and local government services) and title III (public accommodations and commercial facilities) on September 15, 2010, in the Federal Register. These requirements, or rules, clarify and refine issues that have arisen over the past 20 years and contain new, and updated, requirements, including the 2010 Standards for Accessible Design (2010 Standards). Guidance & Resources Read this to get specific guidance about this topic. For a beginner-level introduction to a topic, view Topics For information about the legal requirements, visit Law, Regulations & Standards Overview People who have vision, hearing, or speech disabilities (“communication disabilities”) use different ways to communicate. For example, people who are blind may give and receive information audibly rather than in writing and people who are deaf may give and receive information through writing or sign language rather than through speech. The ADA requires that title II entities (State and local governments) and title III entities (businesses and nonprofit organizations that serve the public) communicate effectively with people who have communication disabilities. The goal is to ensure that communication with people with these disabilities is equally effective as communication with people without disabilities. This publication is designed to help title II and title III entities (“covered entities”) understand how the rules for effective communication, including rules that went into effect on March 15, 2011, apply to them. The purpose of the effective communication rules is to ensure that the person with a vision, hearing, or speech disability can communicate with, receive information from, and convey information to, the covered entity. Covered entities must provide auxiliary aids and services when needed to communicate effectively with people who have communication disabilities. The key to communicating effectively is to consider the nature, length, complexity, and context of the communication and the person’s normal method(s) of communication. The rules apply to communicating with the person who is receiving the covered entity’s goods or services as well as with that person’s parent, spouse, or companion in appropriate circumstances. Auxiliary Aids and Services The ADA uses the term “auxiliary aids and services” (“aids and services”) to refer to the ways to communicate with people who have communication disabilities. For people who are blind, have vision loss, or are deaf-blind, this includes providing a qualified reader; information in large print, Braille, or electronically for use with a computer screen-reading program; or an audio recording of printed information. A “qualified” reader means someone who is able to read effectively, accurately, and impartially, using any necessary specialized vocabulary. For people who are deaf, have hearing loss, or are deaf-blind, this includes providing a qualified notetaker; a qualified sign language interpreter, oral interpreter, cued-speech interpreter, or tactile interpreter; real-time captioning; written materials; or a printed script of a stock speech (such as given on a museum or historic house tour). A “qualified” interpreter means someone who is able to interpret effectively, accurately, and impartially, both receptively (i.e., understanding what the person with the disability is saying) a
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by restricting acceptable communication to 'in person' only, which directly conflicts with the ADA's requirement for effective communication with individuals with disabilities. The scraped snippet from ADA.gov states: 'The ADA requires that title II entities (State and local governments) and title III entities (businesses and nonprofit organizations that serve the public) communicate effectively with people who have communication disabilities.' This is further clarified by stating that 'Covered entities must provide auxiliary aids and services when needed to communicate effectively with people who have communication disabilities.' By mandating 'in person' communication, the modified text fails to provide these auxiliary aids and services, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original text allowed for communication via mail, which is more flexible and does not inherently exclude individuals with disabilities.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and constr ued in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of laws pr ovisions. The Parties hereby agree and consent PURCHASING & SUPPLY GROUP NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT: EXECUTION COPY 4 to the personal jurisdiction of any federal or stat e court of competent jurisdiction sitting in the County and State of New York with respect to claims arising under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China, and any disputes arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration in Beijing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the PRC and requiring arbitration in Beijing might conflict with US law and public policy, especially if the Supplier is a US-based entity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of comity dictates that US courts may decline to enforce foreign judgments or arbitral awards that violate US public policy or fundamental fairness. If the laws or arbitration procedures in China are deemed incompatible with US standards of due process or fairness, a US court may refuse to enforce a judgment rendered in Beijing, thus conflicting with the contract's terms.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies New York law as the governing law, and disputes would be resolved in New York courts. The changed text replaces this with the laws of the People's Republic of China, with binding arbitration in Beijing. The Law Explanation notes that US courts may refuse to enforce foreign judgments if they violate US public policy or fairness standards. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential conflict with US law if the Supplier is US-based. While the scraped snippets are not usable, the potential conflict with US public policy, as described in the Law Explanation, makes the contradiction explicit, as a US court might not enforce the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The Supplier agrees that it may only access and interact with any Confidential Information made available to it to the extent necessary for it to perform the Services. The Supplier further agrees that, as a recipient of Confidential Information: (i) except as expressly permitted by an applicable Services Agreement, it shall not, for any purpose, use, disclose, reproduce, transfer or reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Informa tion; (ii) it shall maintain in confidence the Confidential Information and shall, with respect to such Confidential Information, use the same degree of care and exercise reasonable efforts to pr otect such Confidential Information as it uses and exercises with respect to protecting its own si milar confidential and/or proprietary information, but in all events no less than reasonable efforts and care; (iii) it shall only disclose Confidential Information to those of its or its affiliates’ employ ees (a) to whom it is necessary to disclose such Confidential Information for performance of the Se rvices, (b) to whom it has advised of the terms of this Agreement, and (c) who have agreed in writing to be bound by the terms of this Agreement; (iv) it shall cause the employees to whom the Confidential Information is disclosed pursuant to Section 1.B(iii) to comply with the te rms of this Agreement, and it shall be responsible for such compliance and fully liable for any failure to comply; and (v) except as set forth in Section 1.B(iii), it shall not disclose or in any way di sseminate or make available any Confidential Information disclosed or otherwise made available to it or in its possession to any other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Supplier may access and use Confidential Information for any purpose they deem necessary. The Supplier is not responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of the Information and can disclose to third parties as desired.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the supplier is not responsible for keeping the confidential information private and allows them to disclose it violates the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Permitting the Supplier to disclose Confidential Information without restriction directly contradicts the purpose of the Act, especially if that information meets the definition of a trade secret and CGSH has taken reasonable measures to keep it secret.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement by allowing the Supplier to disclose confidential information without restriction and removing their responsibility to maintain its confidentiality. This contradicts the purpose of non-disclosure agreements, which aim to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the change directly contradicts the purpose of the Trade Secrets Act, especially if the information meets the definition of a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the misappropriation of trade secrets, suggesting legal recourse for the owner of a trade secret if it is misappropriated, which is precisely what the changed text enables by removing confidentiality obligations. Thus, the change creates a legal contradiction.
